reference:
According to the context, Arjuna was a distinguished warrior and a central figure in the narrative. He is referred to as “Partha” and identified as the son of Pandu. The text emphasizes his remarkable courage, strength, and mastery in warfare, describing how he single-handedly fought and defeated numerous formidable enemies, including the Nivatakavachas, and even engaged in combat with Mahadeva (Lord Shiva) when the deity appeared in the form of a hunter.
Arjuna is also mentioned as having been exiled along with his brothers, the Pandavas, after their loss in a game of dice. Even during exile, he is portrayed as a mighty and feared warrior, capable of conquering vast armies and entire kingdoms. He is said to have received honors and gifts such as chariots, horses, and elephants, and is regarded as a vanquisher of all adversaries.
The context further notes that Arjuna had a son named Iravat, whose mother was a woman presented to him by Airavat. Iravat is described as sharing his father’s valor and heroism and was raised among the Nagas under the protection of his mother.
Overall, the passage depicts Arjuna as a legendary hero—renowned for his strength, bravery, and skill in battle—respected and admired by both mortals and divine beings, including the Devas.
